name: python
context: Python language ecosystem — packaging, environments, async, typing, testing, and agent development patterns
info: The Python ecosystem covers the language, runtime, and tooling for building AI agents and automation. Python is the dominant language for AI/ML work due to its extensive library ecosystem, readable syntax, and first-class support from Anthropic, OpenAI, LangChain, and every major AI framework. Agent developers need fluency in virtual environments (venv, conda), package management (pip, poetry, uv), async concurrency (asyncio, async/await), type annotations (typing module, mypy, Pydantic), and testing (pytest). Python 3.10+ is the practical baseline for modern agent work: structural pattern matching (3.10), exception groups and TaskGroups (3.11), improved error messages and f-string flexibility (3.12), and free-threaded mode and improved REPL (3.13). The CPython interpreter runs everywhere, and pyproject.toml has replaced setup.py as the standard project configuration format.
triggers: which python version should I use
body:
# Python Ecosystem

Python is the primary language for AI agent development. Its ecosystem spans the interpreter, packaging tools, environment isolation, concurrency primitives, type safety, and testing frameworks.

## Python Versions

Use Python 3.10+ for agent work. Each version adds capabilities that matter:

| Version | Release | Key Features for Agents |
|---------|---------|------------------------|
| 3.10 | Oct 2021 | Structural pattern matching (`match`/`case`), `ParamSpec`, union types `X \| Y` |
| 3.11 | Oct 2022 | `ExceptionGroup`, `asyncio.TaskGroup`, 10-60% faster CPython, `tomllib` |
| 3.12 | Oct 2023 | Improved f-strings (nested quotes), per-interpreter GIL (subinterpreters), `type` statement |
| 3.13 | Oct 2024 | Free-threaded mode (no GIL, experimental), improved REPL with colors, `copy.replace()` |

```bash
# Check installed version
python3 --version

# Use pyenv to manage multiple versions
pyenv install 3.12.4
pyenv local 3.12.4

# Verify interpreter path
which python3
python3 -c "import sys; print(sys.executable)"
```

## Package Management

Three tools dominate Python packaging. Choose based on project needs:

| Tool | Speed | Lockfile | Env Mgmt | Publish | Best For |
|------|-------|----------|----------|---------|----------|
| **[[python/pip]]** | Moderate | `requirements.txt` (manual) | No (use with venv) | `twine` | Simple scripts, quick installs |
| **[[python/poetry]]** | Moderate | `poetry.lock` (auto) | Yes (built-in) | Yes | Libraries, apps needing reproducibility |
| **[[python/uv]]** | Very fast | `uv.lock` (auto) | Yes (built-in) | Yes | All projects (Rust-based, 10-100x faster) |

### pip

```bash
# Install packages
pip install requests anthropic

# Install from requirements file
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Freeze current environment
pip freeze > requirements.txt

# Install in editable mode for development
pip install -e ".[dev]"
```

### poetry

```bash
# Create new project
poetry new my-agent

# Add dependencies
poetry add anthropic httpx
poetry add --group dev pytest mypy ruff

# Install all dependencies
poetry install

# Run commands in the poetry environment
poetry run python main.py
poetry run pytest
```

### uv

```bash
# Create new project (creates pyproject.toml + .venv)
uv init my-agent
cd my-agent

# Add dependencies (resolves and installs in seconds)
uv add anthropic httpx
uv add --dev pytest mypy ruff

# Run commands (auto-creates venv if needed)
uv run python main.py
uv run pytest

# Sync environment from lockfile
uv sync

# Install a standalone Python
uv python install 3.12
```

## Virtual Environments

Never install packages globally. Always isolate project dependencies.

```bash
# Create with venv (stdlib)
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate   # Linux/macOS
.venv\Scripts\activate      # Windows

# Create with uv (faster)
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate

# Deactivate
deactivate
```

See [[python/venv]] for activation patterns, `.venv` conventions, and IDE integration.

## Project Configuration — pyproject.toml

The standard for Python project metadata. All modern tools read it.

```toml
[project]
name = "my-agent"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "An AI agent built with Claude"
requires-python = ">= 3.11"
dependencies = [
    "anthropic>=0.40.0",
    "httpx>=0.27.0",
    "pydantic>=2.0",
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
dev = [
    "pytest>=8.0",
    "mypy>=1.10",
    "ruff>=0.6",
]

[project.scripts]
my-agent = "my_agent.cli:main"

[tool.pytest.ini_options]
testpaths = ["tests"]
asyncio_mode = "auto"

[tool.mypy]
strict = true
python_version = "3.11"

[tool.ruff]
target-version = "py311"
line-length = 100

[tool.ruff.lint]
select = ["E", "F", "I", "UP", "B", "SIM"]
```

## Async Python

Agent code is inherently async — waiting on API calls, tool execution, and I/O.

```python
import asyncio
import httpx

async def fetch_data(url: str) -> dict:
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        response = await client.get(url)
        return response.json()

async def main():
    # Run tasks concurrently with TaskGroup (3.11+)
    async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
        task1 = tg.create_task(fetch_data("https://api.example.com/a"))
        task2 = tg.create_task(fetch_data("https://api.example.com/b"))
    print(task1.result(), task2.result())

asyncio.run(main())
```

See [[python/async]] for patterns: semaphores, timeouts, `async for`, `async with`, and structured concurrency.

## Type Annotations

Type hints make agent code self-documenting and catch bugs before runtime.

```python
from typing import Any
from collections.abc import AsyncIterator
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ToolResult:
    name: str
    output: str | None = None
    error: str | None = None

async def call_tools(
    tools: list[ToolResult],
    timeout: float = 30.0,
) -> AsyncIterator[ToolResult]:
    for tool in tools:
        yield tool
```

See [[python/typing]] for generics, `Protocol`, `TypeVar`, Pydantic models, and `mypy` configuration.

## Testing

pytest is the standard. Agent tests need async support and mocking of external APIs.

```python
import pytest
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, patch

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_agent_tool_call():
    mock_client = AsyncMock()
    mock_client.messages.create.return_value.content = [
        {"type": "text", "text": "result"}
    ]
    result = await run_agent(client=mock_client, prompt="test")
    assert result == "result"
    mock_client.messages.create.assert_called_once()
```

See [[python/testing]] for fixtures, parametrize, coverage, and snapshot testing.

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `PYTHONPATH` | Additional directories to search for modules |
| `PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE` | Set to `1` to skip `.pyc` file generation |
| `PYTHONUNBUFFERED` | Set to `1` for unbuffered stdout/stderr (important in containers) |
| `VIRTUAL_ENV` | Path to active virtual environment (set by activate script) |
| `PIP_INDEX_URL` | Override default PyPI index |
| `UV_CACHE_DIR` | Override uv cache location |
| `PYTHONASYNCIODEBUG` | Set to `1` for asyncio debug mode |
| `PYTHONSTARTUP` | Script to run on interpreter startup |

## REPL and Quick Prototyping

```bash
# Standard REPL
python3

# Enhanced REPL (Python 3.13+ has colors and multiline editing)
python3 -q

# IPython for richer interactive sessions
pip install ipython
ipython

# Run a module directly
python3 -m http.server 8000
python3 -m json.tool data.json
python3 -m venv .venv
```

## Agent Development Stack

A typical AI agent project uses this stack:

```
my-agent/
  pyproject.toml          # Project config and dependencies
  .venv/                  # Virtual environment (git-ignored)
  src/
    my_agent/
      __init__.py
      agent.py            # Core agent logic
      tools.py            # Tool definitions
      prompts.py          # Prompt templates
  tests/
    test_agent.py
    test_tools.py
    conftest.py           # Shared fixtures
  .python-version         # Pin Python version (pyenv)
```

Key dependencies for agent projects:

```bash
uv add anthropic          # Claude API client
uv add httpx              # Async HTTP client
uv add pydantic           # Data validation and settings
uv add mcp                # MCP server/client SDK
uv add --dev pytest pytest-asyncio mypy ruff
```

sections.title: Package management
sections.body: Python has three major package managers: pip (the standard, ships with Python), poetry (dependency management with lockfiles and publishing), and uv (Rust-based, 10-100x faster than pip). All read from pyproject.toml. pip uses requirements.txt for pinning. poetry and uv generate lockfiles automatically. For new projects, uv is the recommended default — it handles environment creation, dependency resolution, and Python version management in a single tool. See [[python/pip]], [[python/poetry]], [[python/uv]] for detailed usage.

sections.title: Virtual environments
sections.body: Virtual environments isolate project dependencies from the system Python and from other projects. The stdlib venv module creates environments. uv and poetry can also manage environments automatically. The convention is to name the environment directory .venv and git-ignore it. Activation sets PATH and VIRTUAL_ENV so python and pip point to the isolated copies. IDEs like VS Code and PyCharm detect .venv automatically. See [[python/venv]] for activation scripts, shell integration, and troubleshooting.

sections.title: Language features for agents
sections.body: Agent code relies on several Python language features: asyncio for concurrent I/O (awaiting API calls, running tools in parallel), type annotations for self-documenting interfaces and catching errors with mypy, dataclasses and Pydantic for structured data, context managers for resource cleanup, decorators for tool registration, and generators/async generators for streaming responses. Python 3.11+ added TaskGroup for structured concurrency and exception groups for handling multiple failures. See [[python/async]] and [[python/typing]].

sections.title: Agent development patterns
sections.body: Building AI agents in Python follows a common pattern: define tools as typed functions, register them with an API client or MCP server, run an event loop that sends prompts and dispatches tool calls, and test with mocked API responses. The key libraries are anthropic (Claude API), mcp (Model Context Protocol), httpx (async HTTP), and pydantic (validation). Project layout uses src-layout with pyproject.toml, and testing uses pytest with pytest-asyncio for async test functions. See [[python/testing]] for testing strategies.

related.note: The Anthropic Python SDK is the primary client for building Claude-based agents
MCP's Python SDK uses asyncio, typing, and Pydantic for server and client development
Node.js is the other major ecosystem for AI agent development alongside Python

author: dojo-community
